other_observers: none. I tried calling for Mark and Dan, but the bird departed as soon as I hollered;
description: Dan and Mark had pished in a nice little flock of birds that weren't sparrows, our first such flock of the day, at about 8 am. I came up behind them as they were pretty much finished with the flock. As they wandered off in search of new prey, I started pishing. I had a steady trickle of new individuals arrivimg, so kept at the pish, squeak, owl-imitation bit.   After about 5 minutes (truly), an Orange-crowned Warbler came into view followed shortly by a vireo. The vireo was entirely clean yellow beneath, brightest on chest and throat (I'd rate as moderate intensity there). There was no streaking or other marks below. The head showed a gray crown, pale yellowish supercilium, dark line through eye (so lores dark). There was a weak pale crescent beneath the eye, not nearly as marked as that of a Warbling Vireo. The bird was relatively small billed for a vireo, but the bill was still hefty compared to that of the OCWA. The bird lacked wingbars. Back color not seen well.Behaviors: Feeding ever so slowly 10-25 feet up in cottonwood and other trees, loosely associated with RCKIs, OCWAs.Call: not heardPlumage: not determined
